name: Nereids
description: Nereids are sea nymphs in Greek mythology, daughters of Nereus and Doris. They are typically depicted as beautiful, graceful, and benevolent beings who dwell in the Mediterranean Sea. Numbering fifty according to most accounts, they are known for assisting sailors in distress and possessing the power to calm the waves. Each Nereid embodies a particular aspect of the sea, and they are often associated with specific locations or natural phenomena within the marine environment. They are frequently seen accompanying Poseidon, the god of the sea, and other marine deities. The Nereids are considered protectors of sailors and fishermen, offering guidance and assistance in times of need. Their beauty and connection to the sea have made them a popular subject in art and literature throughout history, representing the allure and power of the ocean. They are considered immortal, embodying the eternal nature of the sea. They are known for their melodic voices and often sing and dance in the waves. While generally benevolent, they can also be formidable if angered or disrespected. They represent the beauty, grace, and unpredictable nature of the sea.
subtitle: Benevolent Sea Nymphs of the Mediterranean
abilities: Control over the sea and waves
appearance: Nereids are typically depicted as beautiful young women with long, flowing hair, often adorned with pearls or seashells. They are usually depicted as partially nude or wearing light, flowing garments that blend seamlessly with the water. Some accounts portray them with fish tails, while others depict them with human legs. Their skin is often described as luminous and fair, reflecting the light of the sea. They possess captivating eyes that mirror the depths of the ocean.
habitat: The Nereids inhabit the Mediterranean Sea, specifically dwelling in grottoes, caves, and underwater palaces. They are often found near coastlines, islands, and areas frequented by sailors and fishermen. They are also said to reside in the depths of the sea, where they attend to the needs of marine life and maintain the balance of the ocean's ecosystem.
behavior: Nereids are generally benevolent and helpful creatures, known for their kindness towards sailors and fishermen. They are often depicted as playful and carefree, enjoying dancing and singing in the waves. They are fiercely protective of marine life and will intervene to prevent harm to the ocean's ecosystem. While generally peaceful, they can be formidable if angered or disrespected, unleashing the power of the sea against those who threaten their domain.
weaknesses: Loss of connection to the sea
Disrespect or pollution of their marine environment

keyMyths.title: Thetis and Peleus
keyMyths.description: Thetis, a Nereid, was destined to bear a son greater than his father. To prevent this, Zeus forced her to marry the mortal Peleus, resulting in the birth of Achilles. The wedding was a significant event, attended by many gods.

keyMyths.title: Perseus and Andromeda
keyMyths.description: Cassiopeia, queen of Ethiopia, boasted that she was more beautiful than the Nereids. As punishment, Poseidon sent a sea monster to ravage the land, which was only appeased by sacrificing Andromeda to the monster. Perseus rescued her.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: Provides a genealogy of the Nereids and their parents, Nereus and Doris.
sources.text: Provides a genealogy of the Nereids and their parents, Nereus and Doris.

sources.author: Homer
sources.description: Mentions Thetis, a prominent Nereid, and her role in assisting Achilles.
sources.text: Mentions Thetis, a prominent Nereid, and her role in assisting Achilles.

sources.author: Apollodorus
sources.description: Offers various accounts of the Nereids and their involvement in different myths.
